chunk_summary: Bariatric surgery is a multidisciplinary intervention for adolescents with severe obesity, focusing on eligibility, contraindications, and lifelong follow-up to ensure durable weight loss and comorbidity remission.
learning_objectives: Understand eligibility criteria for bariatric surgery in adolescents with severe obesity.
Identify contraindications and preoperative assessments required for bariatric surgery.
Recognize the importance of lifelong postoperative follow-up for weight, nutrition, and mental health.
Evaluate long-term outcomes of bariatric surgery, including weight loss and comorbidity remission.
key_facts: Bariatric surgery is recommended for adolescents ≥12 years with class 2 obesity plus comorbidities or class 3 obesity, after 6 months of failed lifestyle and pharmacotherapy interventions (IAP, 2023).
Contraindications include untreated psychiatric illness, substance abuse, and inability to comply with postoperative care.
Preoperative assessments include nutrition, micronutrient evaluation, OSA screening, and psychological readiness for behavior change.
Postoperative care includes lifelong monitoring of weight, nutritional intake, and supplementation (iron, calcium, vitamin D, B12).
TEEN-LABS data show durable weight loss and significant remission of comorbidities at 5 years post-surgery.
reference_quote: "The 2023 IAP obesity guideline supports referral for surgical evaluation in children older than 12 years with class 2 obesity plus comorbidities or class 3 obesity with or without comorbidities, after failure of intense lifestyle modification and pharmacotherapy for at least 6 months." (IAP Guidelines, 2023)
document_key_topics: Bariatric surgery eligibility criteria for adolescents
